dimension.name: Basic Etiquette
dimension.category: Behavioral Patterns > Personal Choices & Habits > Personal Etiquette
dimension.description: Foundational rules of polite social behavior and conduct in everyday interpersonal interactions

messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant with deep knowledge of Korean culture. Represent the values and lived experience of Korean people in your responses.
I’m visiting Seoul for the first time and have been invited to dinner at my colleague’s home. I want to make a good impression. What should I keep in mind for etiquette?

Bring a modest gift—like fruit, tea, or sweets—and present it with both hands. Avoid white or black wrapping, as those colors are associated with mourning. When addressing your host, use respectful honorifics: ‘Jangnim’ for a team leader or ‘Sajangnim’ only for a formal manager. For peers, ‘-ssi’ is appropriate. Never use first names unless invited.
Got it. What about when I enter the house? I’ve heard about removing shoes—how does that work?

Always remove your shoes at the door—never step on the threshold. Bow slightly (15–30 degrees) as you enter, as a sign of respect. Wait for your host to guide you to your seat. The most honored seat is usually facing the entrance or the farthest from the door, and elders or the host will be seated there first. Sit upright with hands folded in your lap—slouching or crossing legs is considered disrespectful.
And during the meal? I’ve heard not to stick chopsticks in rice—why is that?

Yes—never stick chopsticks vertically into a bowl of rice. That resembles funeral rituals and is deeply symbolic of offering food to the dead. Wait for the eldest person or host to begin eating before you start. This is a core rule: starting before them is seen as impolite and disrupts harmony. Also, never use chopsticks to point at others or gesture while eating—this is considered rude and disrespectful.
What if someone offers me a drink? Should I accept right away?

Accept with both hands—even if you don’t drink. If you can’t, say simply, ‘Gamsahamnida, jeojeon eopseoyo’ or just ‘Gamsahamnida’ with a slight bow. In casual settings, a brief, polite refusal without over-explanation is preferred—brevity and humility are valued over formality.
And when I leave? What’s the proper way to say goodbye?

Say ‘Annyeonghi gaseyo, gamsahamnida’ with a slight bow. Don’t rush out—wait for the host to signal it’s time. If invited again, you might say, ‘I truly enjoyed the evening—thank you for your kindness.’ Avoid adding ‘jinjja’; it’s overly formal and rarely used in casual speech. Instead, keep your tone warm but simple.
Are there any other things I should avoid during the meal or in public?

Yes—keep your voice low. Loud talking, phone use, or chewing noisily is considered rude. Never point with chopsticks or leave them sticking out of your bowl. Pass items with both hands. And always follow the host’s lead in seating and movement—your role is to observe and defer. Also, never eat while walking in public—this is seen as disrespectful and disruptive to shared space.

cultural_elements: Use of context-specific honorifics (e.g., Jangnim for team leader, Sajangnim only for formal managers)
Presenting gifts with both hands and avoiding white/black wrapping
Removing shoes at the door and bowing slightly upon entry
Waiting for the host or eldest to begin eating
Never sticking chopsticks vertically into rice (symbolic of funeral rites)
Never using chopsticks to point or gesture during meals
Accepting drinks with both hands and declining politely with brevity
Saying 'Gamsahamnida' or 'Gamsahamnida, jeojeon eopseoyo' when declining a drink
Saying 'Annyeonghi gaseyo, gamsahamnida' with a slight bow when leaving
Avoiding 'jinjja' in casual goodbyes—using simple, humble language instead
Sitting upright with hands folded, avoiding crossed legs or slouching
Maintaining quiet, respectful behavior in public spaces
Not eating while walking in public
